title: Information Revelation in Decentralized Markets
abstract: How does information get revealed in decentralized markets? We test several hypotheses inspired by recent dealer-network theory. To do so, we construct an empirical map of information revelation where two dealers are connected based on the synchronicity of their quote changes. The tests, based on the euro to Swiss franc spot rate (EUR/CHF) quote data including the 2015 crash, largely support theory: strongly connected (i.e., central) dealers are more informed. Connections are weaker when there is less to be learned. The crash serves to identify how a network forms when dealers are transitioned from no-learning to learning, that is, from a fixed to a floating rate.

article.introduction:
Technology facilitates the entry ofelectronic markets that are cheap to set up and easy to tailor to various clienteles. New markets successfully compete with incumbent ones. Many dealers, for example, have started their own markets. As a result, securities are increasingly traded in decentralized markets. This trend holds for all asset classes (Johnson (2010, Chapters 2,3)).
New market entry delivers well-known benefits of competition (e.g., lower fees and better products), yet might be socially costly. The reason is that end-users become dispersed across markets, which potentially inhibits efficient re-allocations, as decentralized markets lead to costly cross-market search for efficient trades. A relatively recent and growing literature focuses on such friction (e.g., Hollifield, Neklyudov, and Spatt (2017), Maggio, Kermani, and Song (2017), Li and Schürhoff (2019)).
Relatively unexplored, however, is the question of how (fundamental) information gets revealed in decentralized markets.1With dispersed trading, information is produced in local markets and somehow is aggregated across them. The literature on how exactly this happens is young and, to the best of our knowledge, only theoretical.
In this paper, we contribute to this literature by testing several hypotheses on information revelation. Motivated by theory, we address the following questions. Do some markets (dealers) specialize endogenously in terms of connecting to many others (Farboodi, Jarosch, and Shimer (2017))? Do these markets benefit by being more informed (Babus and Kondor (2018))? And, in the process of becoming informed, do they charge a higher bid-ask spread to recoup the cost of adverse-selection (similar to the classic market maker of Glosten and Milgrom (1985))? Finally, are connections stronger when there is more to be learned due to more intensive search (Duffie, Malamud, and Manso (2009))?
To test our hypotheses, we develop an approach to mapping information revelation empirically. An important necessary feature of this approach is that it recognizes that price responses to shocks could diverge across markets in the short term, but not in the long term; arbitrage activity disciplines price responses to converge eventually. The challenge is to appropriately characterize price dynamics between the shock and the eventual convergence. Following the de facto standard set by Hasbrouck (1995), we characterize such price dynamics using a vector error-correction model.
The key innovation of our approach is to characterize information revelation and how it interacts with market connections based on what can be thought of as amultivariateimpulse response function (MIRF). In particular, we study how prices in all markets respond to price shocks that transpire in all markets simultaneously (i.e., multivariate reponses to multivariate shocks). We believe that analyzing how these responses change with the time elapsed since a shock captures the process through which information is revealed.2
We consider it economically insightful to create a network map based on bilateral price synchronicity. Consider, for example, two dealers who meet and agree to share their information on a recent shock (as in, for example, Duffie, Malamud, and Manso (2009)). The result is that their quote responses to this shock become more (partially) correlated, which we visualize as a stronger connection (edge) between the two dealers. At the same time, both dealers' quotes become more efficient, which is displayed as them moving closer to the center of the map. In this way, a sequence of dealer meetings yields a series of information-revelation maps (a movie) that characterizes the process through which information gets revealed in the dealer community.
Diagnostic analysis of a (network) graph based on correlations has proven useful in a variety of contexts, such as DNA arrays or Internet traffic (see de la Fuente et al. (2004) and Pastor-Satorras, Vázquez, and Vespignani (2001), respectively). Perhaps closest to our work is the use of network graphs to study information processing in neuroscience. In particular, Achard et al. (2006) construct a network of various parts of the brain based on correlations in magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) scans to study how a brain processes information. A parallel line of research in neuroscience focuses on uncovering the brain's hardwiring. The nervous system of a roundworm was the first to be completely mapped out (Achacoso and Yamamoto (1992)). In a sense, our paper resembles the MRI study through its focus on co-activity, whereas earlier dealer-network studies are like the roundworm study focused on hardwiring, as they analyzephysicalconnections between dealers based on whether they trade with one another. We believe the two lines of research are complementary.
We analyze information revelation for a sample of nine quote streams in the euro to Swiss franc spot rate (EUR/CHF) in 2015. The data include quote streams of eight major foreign exchange (FX) dealers as well as that of an interdealer limit-order book, namely, Electronic Broking Services (EBS). Because the dealers can effectively be considered markets, we refer to them interchangeably as dealers or markets throughout (see SectionIV.Afor a more detailed discussion of the market structure). The sample also includes news data to identify intervals “treated” with public information.
Our key empirical findings are as follows. First, the information-revelation maps are nontrivial and stable over time. The maps reveal that dealers differ considerably in their number of dealer connections. Second, connections are assortative and hence the network exhibits a core-periphery structure. Third, the strongly connected dealers, henceforth referred to as central dealers, are more informed in two respects: their price-quote innovations are more revealing of an informational shock contemporaneously, and they respond to the full shock more quickly.3Fourth, if part of the information becomes public through news, then dealer connections become weaker and information is revealed more slowly. This result confirms the theoretical prediction that making some of the information public reduces private search in the network (e.g., Duffie, Malamud, and Manso (2009)).4
The above findings are based on EUR/CHF trading in the two weeks after the Swiss franc peg to the euro was removed on January 15, 2015. However, the sample also includes the day of this removal, when at 10:30 AM local time the Swiss central bank made the surprise announcement. The euro crashed against the franc in the minutes thereafter, one of the most salient crashes in recent history (Breedon et al. (2018)).
In a sense, the January 15 event, which we refer to as the “EUR/CHF crash,” provides us with a natural experiment with respect to how a market structure emerges. The dealer community was exogenously transitioned from not learning to learning the EUR/CHF exchange rate. Analysis of dealer quote streams on that day yields the following insights. The market converged to a centralized one (i.e., EBS only) right after the announcement and gradually transitioned to a decentralized one in the afternoon. The network maps illustrate such transition, with the late-afternoon map correlating strongly with the steady-state map observed in the weeks thereafter. This pattern is in line with the Farboodi, Jarosch, and Shimer (2017) equilibrium in which dealers specialize endogenously with only some becoming highly connected.
The paper proceeds as follows. SectionIoutlines our hypotheses based on theoretical literature. SectionIIintroduces our empirical approach, and establishes key measures of information revelation networks. SectionIIIprovides implementation details and defines network characteristics. SectionIVpresents the empirical application, including a review of learning in FX markets as well as an overview of the sample data. SectionVpresents results of our empirical hypothesis tests. SectionVIconcludes.
article.I. Hypotheses:
Before turning to the empirical analysis, in this section we develop five hypotheses motivated by prior literature. We state them formally below, with each followed by its corresponding motivation. In SectionV, each hypothesis is taken to the data.
Hypothesis 1.The bilateral (price) connections that form the dealer network are nontrivial and stable (as opposed to dealers being matched randomly).

A more recent class of models shows how agents specialize, which endogenously leads to a stable core-periphery structure. Some models show that ex ante heterogeneity in asset valuations makes some agents specialize and become de facto intermediaries (e.g., Afonso and Lagos (2015), Hugonnier, Lester, and Weill (2015)). Others show that network externalities can generate heterogeneity ex post for agents who are identical ex ante (e.g., Farboodi, Jarosch, and Shimer (2017), Wang (2017)).
Two more observations on testing this hypothesis are worth noting. First, we caution that rejecting random matching does not take away from the economic insights that such models generate. Second, random matching cannot be rejected prima facie. One way in which two dealers are “matched” is if a client that they have in common requests a quote from both. The event of getting or not getting the trade teaches each dealer about the price that the other dealer quoted, and therefore is likely to generate a signal about the information the other dealer has (see SectionIV.Afor a more detailed discussion on how one might interpret random matching in the context of FX trading).
Hypothesis 2.Central dealers are more informed.
Hypothesis 3.Central dealers charge higher spreads.

Babus and Kondor (2018) do not have predictions on the quoted bid-ask spread because they abstract from market structure by invoking a Walrasian auctioneer. In real-world markets, an important source of information for dealers is the order flow they receive on their quotes, that is, they learn from being adversely selected on their quotes. The more information asymmetry there is, the wider they set their quotes to recoup adverse selection losses. This result is well established in the literature (e.g., Glosten and Milgrom (1985)). Applying this insight to our decentralized market structure suggests that central dealers are more exposed to adverse selection cost because they interact with many others (and as a result become more informed). This motivates Hypothesis 3.
Alternatively, central dealers might have superior information beyond what they learn from their order flow, or they might set a tighter quote to attract more flow and thus learn more quickly. We revisit these alternative explanations when we test this hypothesis in SectionV.C.
Hypothesis 4.If there is less information to be learned, then dealer connections become weaker and information is revealed more slowly.

Duffie, Malamud, and Manso (2009) show that if part of the information is revealed publicly, for example, through news, then private incentives to find (the remaining) information decline and search intensity is reduced. This slows the speed of full information revelation. These findings imply that in the empirical network map, dealer connections weaken and it takes longer for quotes in all markets to fully reveal an informational shock. In SectionIV.A, we discuss how one might interpret the model in the context of real-world FX trading.
Hypothesis 5.The steady-state information revelation structure grows out of a “Big Bang.” Revelation starts centrally with one market dominating and gradually evolves into a steady-state decentralized structure.
article.II. Approach:
To characterize information revelation in a decentralized market, we propose an empirical network map. Our approach follows in the footsteps of Hasbrouck (1995), who models price dynamics in a one-security-many-markets setting. This approach explicitly recognizes that market prices are co-integrated in such a way that there is a common (univariate) stochastic trend (i.e., a random walk). Hasbrouck (1995) argues that this trend can be reasonably thought of as anefficient price. Changes in this price capture the arrival of information. Hasbrouck (1995) develops this structure to identify how much each market contributes to such information arrival, which he refers to as a market's information share. He constructs this information share based on the estimates of a standard vector error-correction model (VECM). Following his approach, we use the VECM estimates to not only compute information shares, but also to characterize the process through which information gets revealed in the various markets over time. The remainder of this section describes our approach in detail.
To shed light on the information revelation process, we study the degree of price inefficiency in each market, along with the strength of bilateral connections. These two pieces of information result in a series of network maps indexed by τ (a movie) that graphically illustrate the dynamics of price revelation in the various markets. We now turn to defining such price inefficiency and connections formally.
The price inefficiency measure can be interpreted as the fraction of information that marketihas yet to reveal τ periods after the shock. A few points are worth mentioning. First, by constructionequals 1 just before a shock hits. Second, it converges to 0 when τ is taken to infinity. Third, at the time of the shock,, this measure captures how much of the information embedded in the (multivariate) shock is revealed in marketicontemporaneously. Fourth, although one might expect the measure to decay from at most 1 to 0 when τ progresses, this need not be the case. The measure could temporarily exceed 1 if the price of a market drifts off substantially in the short run. Fifth, there is ample evidence of mispricing in the FX markets. For example, Andersen and Bollerslev (1998) and Ederington and Lee (1993) document heightened volatility levels following macroeconomic announcements, which is consistent with heterogeneously informed agents. Our measure of price inefficiency can potentially capture such heterogeneity. We apply it to public news releases in SectionV.D.
Finally, note that the approach is relatively straightforward to implement as it involves only linear regressions (i.e., VECMs are estimated through regressions). Thus, not only should the approach be attractive to a wide set of researchers, but it might also be the only feasible approach at times when data sets grow “big” (in modern markets quotes can change several timeswithina millisecond; see, for example, Menkveld (2018)).
A key benefit of representing partial correlations as a network is that we can compare this network to earlier evidence on dealer networks. We further need the network characterization to test Hypotheses 2 and 3. For example, does the network show a core-periphery structure? And, how do we measure the centrality of a market in a network? To explore the network structure along these lines, we define two sets of variables. First, the centrality of a market is defined as its edge degree, that is, the number of connections that it has with other markets. One way to measure such degree is by using thresholding as is commonly done in other sciences (e.g., Hero and Rajaratnam (2012)). We defineCentrality0.1as the number of significant edges that a market has in excess of 0.1. We similarly defineCentrality0.2for a threshold 0.2. We further defineCentralityAllas the sum of all significantly positive edge weights, motivated by Barrat et al. (2004).
Second, a network that consists of high- and low-degree markets need not show a core-periphery structure; it will do so only if high-degree markets tend to connect to high-degree markets and low-degree markets tend to connect to low-degree markets. This is known as assortative mixing and graphically such a network resembles a single star. A disassortative network, in contrast, looks like a collection of stars with relatively weak connections between them. To examine whether our network map exhibits a core-periphery structure, we defineAssortCoeffollowing Newman (2002, Equation 3) as the edge-degree correlation among all markets that are connected in an undirected network. A positive value corresponds to assortative mixing, whereas a negative one corresponds to disassortative mixing.
Recent studies propose essentially three alternative network maps for financial securities (Billio et al. (2012), Barigozzi and Brownlees (2014), Diebold and Yılmaz (2014)). The focus of these studies is fundamentally different from ours. In particular, they aim to map theconnectednessof securities, and therefore paint with a much broader brush than we do. We focus instead on the dynamics ofinformation revelationin markets that trade thesamesecurity. The others base a directional edge on, for example, whether the return of one security Granger causes the return of another (Billio et al. (2012)), or whether it Granger causes the return of the otherin the long run(Barigozzi and Brownlees (2014)). Alternatively, they base an edge on the extent to which one security's shocks appear in the forecast variance of another security (Diebold and Yılmaz (2014)). Importantly, none of these approaches apply to our setting in which a single security trades in multiple markets. In econometric terms, in our model prices are co-integrated, whereas in the other models they are not.
article.III. Implementation Details:
Our proposed network map of information revelation is based on two statistical objects: the price inefficiencies determine vertex positions, and the partial correlation matrix is the basis for bilateral links (edge weights). Both objects are nonlinear functions of the VECM error terms () and their short- and long-term impact on price (and ψ, respectively).
The information-revelation measures, such asPriceInefficiencyandCentrality0.1, are functions of the parameter estimates of the VECM model in10. These VECM estimates include, γ, and the covariance matrix of the innovations: Ω. The parameters needed for calculation of the information-revelation measures areand the long-run impact parameter ψ. These parameters are derived by computing the (standard) cumulative impulse response function implied by the VECM parameters (i.e., they are functions of these parameters). For ψ, we verify that a unit shock to one market has the same long-term effect inallmarkets.
We let the price vector underlying the returns and error correction vectors be the average of the quoted bid and ask prices prevailing at the end of each period, known as the “midpoints.” We use the Bayesian information criterion to determine the number of lagsMand estimate the VECM using ordinary least squares. For application-specific estimation notes, see footnote 15.
The price inefficiency and the partial correlation matrix are nonlinear functions of the VECM parameter estimates, and hence their statistical distributions are not easily characterized. For our main application, we address this by cutting the full time series into many shorter subsamples. We reestimate the VECM for each subsample, and thereby obtain a distribution of estimates for each object of interest. An additional benefit is that it makes the procedure feasible on a standard desktop computer. The procedure follows Hasbrouck (2003), who splits a three-month time series into daily subsamples. More generally, it is in the tradition of Bartlett (1950) and Fama and MacBeth (1973).
For example, consider the partial correlation between marketsiandjat time τ. If the estimate in subsamplekis denoted, the overall sample estimate becomes, whereKis the number of subsamples. For statistical inference, one employs a standardt-test based on the mean and standard deviation of theseKobservations (as if these were the only data points one had).
If the system is expected to show time-varying parameters, then pooling the subsample estimates together in a Fama-MacBeth procedure is not appropriate (an example of this is the day of the EUR/CHF crash in our application; see SectionV.E). In such a case, standard errors for a single subsample can be obtained by bootstrapping from the asymptotically normal distribution of the VECM parameter estimates. More specifically, to establish the asymptotic distribution for, one can randomly draw a VECM parameter from its asymptotic distribution and compute the partial correlation. Each drawkthen adds a new estimate, which we denote by. Statistical inference can then be done based on this set of estimates (i.e., Fama and MacBeth (1973)).
In ann-market network, there arepartial correlations to compute. If one does not adjust the significance level for testing individual edges, then, given the large number of potential edges, one should expect some of them to be significant, even if the data-generating network has no edges. To avoid such Type I errors, we follow the Šidák (1967) procedure and base the individual edge significance level on the “family-wide” error rate.7
What remains after defining all network-related measures discussed in SectionIIare measures to characterize the origin and speed of information revelation and the bid-ask spread. These measures are defined in the remainder of this section.
We characterize information revelation by defining two variables. First,InfoSpeedis the average time it takes for a market to incorporate 80% of the information contained in a price shock (i.e., the time τ whenPriceInefficiencydrops below 20%). To make the measureincreasein speed, we multiply it by −1.
Conceptually,InfoSpeedandInfoSharelikely capture different aspects of the information-revelation process. In economic models, agents typically learn by processing private signals and by the trading process itself. For example, Kim and Verrecchia (1994) is an often-cited classic model on how a cross-section of traders learns about a firm's future cash flows. The model combines Kyle (1985) with Admati and Pfleiderer (1988). Importantly, traders learn in two stages. First, they privately (imperfectly) process a firm's accounts and form priors on what these accounts mean for future cash flows. Second, they participate in the market and update their priors based on the order flow of others who, in turn, also processed the firm's accounts. One can think ofInfoShareas potentially a natural measure of the first stage, whereasInfoSpeedlikely measures the second stage.8
To test our hypotheses, we also need the bid-ask spread for each market in each subsample. We defineQuotedSpreadas the bid-ask price differential divided by the spread midpoint, reported as the duration-weighted average across all quote observations in the subsample.
article.IV. Application:
As discussed in the introduction, securities markets for all asset classes appear to converge to a hybrid structure. The structure often combines quote-driven bilateral trading (OTC) and order-driven multilateral trading (limit order books). The FX market is exemplary in this sense. Broker-dealers provide streams of bid and ask quotes to their clients directly or indirectly through multi-broker platforms such as FXAll and State Street's FX Connect and Currenex (Johnson (2010, p. 63)). Broker-dealers further trade with one another through public limit order books operated by, for example, EBS or Reuters. This hybrid nature therefore makes the FX market a perfect laboratory to study information revelation in modern securities markets.9
The way to think about how information gets incorporated into exchange rates has long puzzled researchers. The idea that the main conduit is a stream of public macro news has long been considered incomplete, at best (see, for example, Meese and Rogoff (1983)).
A more recent literature emphasizes order flow as a source of (private) information on exchange rates. Evans and Lyons (2002), for example, find that initiator-signed interdealer order flow explains 60% of daily FX returns. Subsequent research shows that a dealer's customer orders contain information (e.g., Bjønnes and Rime (2005), Menkhoff et al. (2016)) and that a trader's future transactions depend on the information contained in his current trades (e.g., Menkhoff and Schmeling (2010)).
A key arena for interaction and learning in modern FX markets is the multilateral trading facility (MTF). In an MTF, clients can issue an electronic request for quotes (RFQ) from dealers signed up to the system. When issuing an RFQ, the client typically reaches out to three to five dealers of his choosing, then picks one of them and contacts the dealer to negotiate a price for the trade he intends to do. In the process, the client often reveals whether he intends to buy or sell, as many RFQ systems only support one-way quoting (Johnson (2010, p. 42)). Investment Technology Group (ITG (2015)) provides a detailed description of the process.
The interaction through RFQs is rather opaque for dealers as they see only the identity of the client and his RFQ. For example, they do not see how many other dealers participate. Nevertheless, the RFQ process may be viewed as the real-world equivalent of dealers meeting bilaterally in theoretical models (e.g., Duffie, Malamud, and Manso (2009)). For example, the “winner” in a one-sided quote request initiated by a seller learns that his bid was highest and is likely to update his estimate of fundamental value downwards. Similar reasoning implies that other dealers likely update their estimate upwards.
The RFQ process provides a useful lens to interpret our hypotheses in the FX context. For example, if there are essentially only a few active clients who select the same subset of dealers every time they issue an RFQ, then this may induce structure in the dealer information network as discussed in Hypothesis 1.10In contrast, if there are (almost infinitely) many clients who are randomly picked to issue an RFQ, then even if, individually, they select the same subset of dealers, the outcome would resemble random matching between dealers.
Furthermore, if dealers do indeed learn from participation in RFQs, then those who are more active should become more informed, as in Hypothesis 2. RFQ participation, however, is likely to be costly, as putting together a competitive quote requires time and resources. The degree of participation could therefore be interpreted as search intensity, which Hypothesis 4 states should be higher at times when there is more to be learned. The reason for such stronger search intensity in the model by Duffie, Malamud, and Manso (2009) is that an agent is called to “action” as governed by an independent Poisson process. The more uninformed an agent is when he is called to action, the costlier such action is for him. The real-world equivalent for such action process might be that a dealer is contacted privately by a loyal client for a large trade. Poor knowledge of fundamental value costs him dearly when pricing such a trade, either by taking on substantial price risk or by being adversely selected by his client.
We focus on spot trading in the EUR/CHF currency pair in January 2015. The first half of that month is characterized by high price stability as the Swiss National Bank (SNB) maintained a minimum exchange rate for the EUR/CHF (a binding constraint in this period).11However, on January 15, 10:30 AM local time, the SNB announced that it was discontinuing the minimum exchange rate effective immediately, sending the EUR/CHF rate into a free float.
Figure1presents a time line of the three weeks of January 2015 included in our sample. Our main application is based on the two weeks after the EUR/CHF crash, denoted as “baseline sample” in Figure1. This sample consists of all weekdays from January 19 through January 30. To analyze network stability, we also split the baseline sample into two subsamples: January 19 to 23 (Week 1) and January 26 to 30 (Week 2). Finally, we analyze how the structure of information revelation develops over the course of the day of the EUR/CHF crash.
We highlight two key differences between the OTC and the interdealer-platform data. First, the OTC data contain the best bid and ask quote for each dealer but no quote volume or information about transactions. Second, the OTC quotes are not strictly binding. When a client indicates (electronically) that he or she would like to trade at a price quote, then it is up to the dealer to either confirm or reject the proposed trade (see, for example, the RFQ protocol described in SectionIV.A). In the EBS limit order book, in contrast, an order is actionable until canceled.13
The FX market is open for trading around the clock but we base the analysis on 9:00 AM to 5:00 PM local time.14Figure2shows that this is the most active part of the 24-hour period in terms of quote updates, both for EBS and for the OTC quote updates based on the selected eight dealers.
To study information revelation in electronic markets, it is critically important that price data are available at high frequencies. The EBS data are recorded at a 100-millisecond frequency, which is also the highest frequency at which EBS disseminates quote updates to its clients. The OTC data are time stamped to the millisecond but when pooling the data we pick the 100-millisecond frequency to put all markets on equal footing.
We split each trading day into five-minute subsamples. This yields 96 subsamples per trading day, which, for the 10 trading days in the baseline sample, yields 960 estimates in total. We feel that this sample size yields sufficient statistical power to estimate the various objects of interest (e.g., information shares and partial correlations).15
article.V. Results:
In this section, we first present the information-revelation map for the baseline sample. This is followed by five subsections, each of which is devoted to testing one of the five hypotheses outlined in SectionI.
TableIpresents the partial correlation matrix based on market responses half a second after a shock (i.e., forin the MIRF). When we zoom into a single frame of the price-revelation movie, we pickbecause this is where most of the action is. If we pick one that is too early or too late, then no market has moved substantially yet or all markets have moved. The partial correlation matrix is symmetric and hence the table only shows the lower diagonal part. Note that as the network is based on this matrix, we also refer to it as the (network) adjacency matrix.
The adjacency matrix shows that most, but not all, significant edges in the network are positive. We reiterate that these partial correlations are correlations between two markets' price responses after controlling for price responses of all other markets. Overall, the high number of significant edges suggests a strong presence of bilateral informational links across markets. The few instances of negative partial correlations may be due to short-term disagreement across dealers on how to interpret the information that just arrived.
Figure3plots the network maps associated with theadjacency matrix as well as those for. Together these maps illustrate the process through which information is revealed in the markets—we thus consider Figure3the signature figure of the paper. The network maps show that all markets appear to have at least some private information, as none of them is on the red dashed circle at. They appear to become monotonically more efficient as time progresses, albeit at different speeds. And, reassuringly, they all end up at the red diamond in the center at(i.e., after five full seconds).
A striking pattern in Figure3is that markets that are more centrally positioned (as a result of low price inefficiencies) also appear to have higher edge weights (displayed as darker shades of blue). In particular, the relatively central markets DLR1, DLR2, and EBS seem strongly connected throughout. The more peripheral markets in terms of price efficiency (e.g., DLR6 and DLR8) obtain higher edge weights only as they move closer to the center. We return to these observations and test whether they are statistically significant in SectionsV.AandV.B.
TableIIpresents several statistics based on the network maps to further characterize the information-revelation process. The results lead to the following observations. First, EBS appears to dominate information generation as itsInfoShareis 28.51%, followed by DLR1 with a share of 14.02%. In terms of information-revelationspeed, in contrast, DLR1 is the leading market with anInfoSpeedamounting to −22.98. EBS is substantially slower at −29.33 and, in turn, only marginally faster than DLR2 and DLR5, which both have information shares below 5%. Overall, the evidence shows that theInfoShareandInfoSpeedmeasures capture distinct aspects of the information-revelation process.
Second, the network centrality measures show that DLR1 is the most central of the eight dealers. Note further that several of the dealers are more central than the interdealer market EBS. The markets DLR6 and DLR8 have the lowest centrality and may be considered peripheral. The results are consistent across the three centrality measures (which are correlated but not perfect substitutes, as rank correlations are between 0.61 and 0.83).16
Our first hypothesis states that the network is nontrivial and stable. We first test for a core-periphery structure, then, if we find one, we test whether it is stable over time.
To test whether information revelation exhibits a core-periphery structure, we focus on the network's assortativity coefficient. This coefficient is zero under the null of an Erdös-Rényi network (i.e., a random graph), but positive when central markets tend to connect to each other thus forming a (single) core.
Panel A of TableIIIpresents the results. The assortativity coefficients are significantly positive for the binary adjacency matrices underlying bothCentrality0.2andCentrality0.1, thus revealing a core-periphery structure.17This is in line with the core-periphery structure documented for dealer networks based on transactional relationships (e.g., Hollifield, Neklyudov, and Spatt (2017), Li and Schürhoff (2019)). We believe our evidence adds to this literature because the nature of our network is fundamentally different. In particular, the information-revelation network is identified from high-frequency quote streams, whereas the other studies base their network on low-frequency transaction data, as they do not focus on information dynamics.
Panel B of TableIIIpresents results on network stability over time. Stability is tested by splitting the two-week sample into two separate weeks. The network is then estimated separately for each week. The associated adjacency matrices are vectorized to compute the correlation between them.
The results testify to network stability. The correlation across the two weeks is 0.79 and 0.66 when edges are binary and based on thresholded partial correlations (corresponding toCentrality0.2andCentrality0.1, respectively). These correlations are highly statistically significant. For the nonbinary network where edge weights are equal to (positive) partial correlations, the adjacency-matrix correlation across the two weeks is 0.88 and again highly significant. Taken together, these results along with the assortativity results support the hypothesized nontrivial stable network.
Our second hypothesis states that central dealers are better informed. We test this hypothesis in two ways. First, we pick the network map after half a second and verify whether, in the cross-section of dealers, the most central ones are most informed. Second, we explore the dynamics embedded in the MIRFs to study whether dealers do indeed become more informed after becoming more connected.
Figure4contains two scatter plots that illustrate the relationship between a dealer's centrality (Centrality0.2) and his quote informativeness. Panel A plots dealer centrality against how inefficient his price quote is half a second after a price shock (PriceInefficiency). The plot and the associated regression line suggest a strong and statistically significant relationship. Price quotes of more central dealers are more efficient than those of periphery dealers half a second after a shock. This result is also economically significant, as the fraction of information yet to be revealed (i.e.,PriceInefficiency) ranges from approximately 25% for the most central dealer to more than 75% for the most peripheral dealer.
Panel B plots a dealer's centrality against his information share at the time of a price shock (InfoShare). This plot again reveals a strong and statistically significant relationship. More central dealers seem to be more informed at the time of a shock. The result is economically significant, with information shares ranging from about 2% for the most peripheral dealers to 15% for the most central dealer.
Taken together, these scatter plots suggest that a dealer's centrality is positively associated with how informed he is, both contemporaneously and half a second after a shock. Finally, we note that the results of these two plots do not depend on the centrality measure used, as they continue to hold when either one of the two other centrality measures is used (i.e.,Centrality0.1orCentralityAll, see the Internet Appendix18).
Pushing the analysis further, is there a dynamic relationship between a dealer's centrality and his price efficiency? That is, do we find that, in the impulse response functions, when a dealer gets more connected as time progresses after a shock, his quotessubsequentlybecome more efficient? We rely on standard Granger causality tests to verify whether there is such a relationship as well as to test for a reverse relationship (that is, are price-inefficiency changes followed by centrality changes?).
TableIVshows that Granger causality runs from dealer centrality to price efficiency, but not vice versa. The first two columns corresponding to Model 1 present the regression results for the model specified in13and14. The α2estimate is statistically significant and equal to −0.17. A one standard-deviation increase in dealer centrality therefore reduces his price inefficiency by 0.17 standard deviations. Note that this effect is identified over and above the strong persistence that is in the price inefficiency variable (i.e., α1is significantly positive: 0.53). The table further shows that there is no Granger causality in the reverse direction as β1is insignificant.
We reestimate the VAR model for the two other centrality measures,Centrality0.1andCentralityAll, in Models 2 and 3, respectively. The results are qualitatively the same as for Model 1. In sum, we believe these Granger causality results suggest that dealers do indeed learn from becoming more central (possibly through increased RFQ participation) and therefore support Hypothesis 2.
The third hypothesis states that central dealers charge a higher bid-ask spread. To test this hypothesis, Figure5presents a scatter plot of a dealer's centrality (Centrality0.2) against his quoted bid-ask spread. The plot does not show a clear relationship, and this is confirmed by a simple linear regression (depicted by the solid line) where the slope is insignificant. These results do not depend on the centrality measure used, as they continue to hold when either one of the two other centrality measures is used (i.e.,Centrality0.1orCentralityAll; see the Internet Appendix).
If, however, one is willing to consider the most central dealer (DLR1) an exception, then a pattern does seem to emerge. More central dealers do seem to charge a higher spread, in line with the hypothesized relationship. The pattern is also economically significant as the least central dealer's (DLR6) bid-ask spread is about four basis points, whereas the second-most central dealer (DLR7) charges almost double that.
However, the most central dealer (DLR1) charges a low spread, of about four basis points. We can think of two reasons why this might be the case. First, DLR1 has superior information right from the start and, in that sense, is less susceptible to being adversely selected on his quotes. He can afford to quote a tight spread to attract order flow. This conjecture is supported by DLR1 having the highest information share—more than double that of any other dealer (seeInfoSharein TableII).
Second, DLR1's strategy might simply be to quote a tight bid-ask spread to attract order flow and thereby learn information quickly, to benefit in later trades. Such a channel is modeled explicitly in Golosov, Lorenzoni, and Tsyvinki (2014), where agents willingly incur losses in early trades to profit in later trades. We find some corroborative evidence for this story, as DLR1 is indeed the dealer who learns quickest (seeInfoSpeedin TableII).
We conclude that empirical support for 3 is weak, at best. This might not be all that surprising given the inconclusive empirical evidence in other studies. Li and Schürhoff (2019), for example, find that central dealers charge higher spreads based on municipal bond transactions, whereas Hollifield, Neklyudov, and Spatt (2017) find the opposite for the interdealer market on securitizations.
The fourth hypothesis states that if there is more information to be learned, dealers become more strongly connected and information is revealed more quickly. The reason is that dealers are expected to search more intensively in such an environment. This hypothesis is tested by comparing information-revelation maps for news subsamples and no-news subsamples.21The idea is that for news subsamples, some of the information is revealed publicly to all traders. For no-news subsamples, in contrast, all information has to be learned privately. In the comparison, each subsample “treated” with news is paired to a subsample without news but with (close to) equal volatility, computed as realized volatility of EBS midquote returns.
Before presenting results, we clarify the procedure we followed to build the two types of subsamples. News release data are obtained from theTRNAdatabase. We filter news items based on Reuters Instrument Code EURCHF to identify relevant items. Among these, we select only “flash messages,” which are messages that build by arriving in sequences. The first message in a sequence features only a subject line (often in capitals). Subsequent messages have increasingly more content and eventually include text in the message body. Focusing on flash messages guarantees that the news item is truly breakingnewsand not a reposting of earlier news with additional comments by the reporter. As an additional validation, we define subsamples featuring flash messages as news subsamples only if they also feature top-quartile volatility, implying that the news messages are influential. Out of the 960 five-minute subsamples, 68 contain flash news messages and 16 out of these are in the top volatility quartile.
TableV, Panel A, presents results of the news analysis. Note that matching news and no-news subsamples based on EBS midquote volatility appears to have worked well. Volatility is 1.00 basis points per 100 millisecond interval for the news subsample and 0.99 for the no-news sample. The difference is statistically insignificant.
The evidence seems largely supportive of the hypothesized lower search intensity and slower price revelation at the time of news. The search intensity effect is demonstrated by the fact that dealer connections are weaker when the incentives for private search are lower. For example, the sum of positive edge weights for each dealer (as measured byCentralityAll) is on average 10% lower when there is news. The difference is statistically significant, albeit weakly so.
Furthermore, prices seem to reveal full information more slowly in the news subsamples, despite getting a head start due to the public signal. For example, it takes 40.92 periods (i.e., 4.092 seconds) for 80% of the information to be revealed in price quotes when there is news, whereas it takes half that time, 20.37 periods, for subsamples without news. The difference is statistically significant. This is in line with what we find for price efficiency half a second after a shock: 59% of the information has yet to be revealed for the news subsamples, whereas, for the no-news subsamples, this is only 48%. The difference is significant at the 10% confidence level.
Rerunning the analysis separately for EBS and DLR1-8 shows that the results are driven by the OTC market; see Panel B of TableV. EBS does not show any statistically significant differences when comparing news to no-news subsamples. The dealer markets essentially demonstrate the patterns above when analyzing all markets jointly, with statistical significance now being slightly stronger. This result is consistent with the model of Duffie, Malamud, and Manso (2009), which motivated our fourth hypothesis. There are no interdealer markets in that model, only dealers. Note, however, that this result could also be due to low statistical power for the interdealer market test. We have only one such market and eight dealer markets and thus many more observations on the latter.
Our fifth hypothesis states that the steady-state information revelation structure emerges gradually. Revelation starts centrally with one market dominating and others joining in sequentially, growing toward steady-state dynamics. To test this hypothesis, we examine how the structure of information revelation developed over the course of the day after the EUR/CHF crash on January 15, 2015. At 10:30 AM that day, the SNB announced that it would stop the EUR/CHF peg effective immediately. EUR/CHF thus transitioned from essentially zero information to meaningful information revelation.
All results thus far illustrate price revelation in the two (full) weeks after the EUR/CHF crash (see the time line in Figure1). The baseline network maps presented in Figure3at the start of this section, followed by tests for structure and stability in SectionV.A, all suggest that EUR/CHF price dynamics reached a nontrivial and stable state, which we refer to as steady state. In the remainder of this section, we show how such a state was reached.
Before plotting the information-revelation maps for January 15, it is useful to characterize trading that day by presenting some standard trade statistics. A detailed discussion would take us too much off-track here and is therefore relegated to the Appendix. A finding that is worth mentioning, however, is that immediately after the 10:30 AM announcement, dealer markets froze, leaving information revelation to the interdealer platform EBS. Dealer markets gradually joined back in and the first meaningful information-revelation maps can therefore be drawn only after 11:30 AM. The finding that information revelation concentrated in a single market after the EUR/CHF crash testifies to the hypothesized Big-Bang structure—in the beginning, all energy concentrated at a single point.
Figure6illustrates how information revelation developed after 11:30 AM. The network maps presented in the figure differ from the baseline maps in two important ways. First, the maps are not based on five-minute subsamples. Instead, we choose time intervals based on when markets enter and exit. For example, Panel A displays the network based on a 10-minute period (11:30 to 11:40 AM) when only three markets participated. Panel B is based on a 25-minute period featuring four markets, and so on.22Second, we estimate one VECM model for each period. To test whether the edges are statistically significant (and to discard insignificant ones), we simulate 1,000 draws of VECM parameters for each period, as described in SectionIII.B.
Panels A through H show how the information-revelation maps gradually grow toward the steady-state map (which is repeated from Figure3and added as Panel I for convenience). EBS exhibits high price efficiency early on (i.e., it is close to the center), but it is not until 12:30 PM that it becomes connected to many dealer markets and thus central in the price-revelation network. DLR1, the most central dealer in steady state, emerges as a central market as of 1:15 PM. From 2:15 PM onwards, the map starts to show close resemblance to the steady-state map. For example, the dealers who are highly connected and well-informed in the steady state (DLR1, DLR2, DLR5, and DLR7) already have similar positions at 2:15 PM.
The adjacency matrix associated with the map of the last period in the day, 4:15 to 5:00 PM, is highly correlated with that of the steady state; the correlation between the matrices is 0.64 (based on the adjacency matrix corresponding toCentralityAll). Earlier adjacency matrices are also positively correlated with the steady-state matrix, although often to a lesser degree.
Taken together, we consider this sequence of information-revelation maps growing steadily toward steady state to be evidence in support of the Farboodi, Jarosch, and Shimer (2017) equilibrium (echoed by our fifth hypothesis). Dealers specialize endogenously and only some become highly connected.
article.VI. Conclusions:
In this paper, we test five hypotheses on information revelation in decentralized securities markets. To do so, we develop a network representation of how such information gets revealed across markets after price shocks. The network in essence depicts a multivariate impulse response function.
We find that the network is nontrivial, stable, and exhibits a core-periphery structure. Central markets are more informed at the time of the shock and learn its full impact more quickly. If part of the information is announced publicly by means of a news release, then the remainder is learned more slowly. Theory relates this finding to reduced search incentives. In a final analysis, we show how the information-revelation network gradually but rapidly grows toward steady state after markets are transitioned from a state of zero information to one where information needs to be learned.
The empirical approach developed in this paper can be applied beyond FX markets (e.g., equities and corporate bonds). We believe that this is useful as securities markets increasingly fragment and trade occurs in a variety of venues.
What remains on the horizon is to show how the information friction in a dealer network affects market quality at large in a fully dynamic model. How does it intersect with efficient reallocations? Interestingly, Babus and Kondor (2018) show formally that in their static setting, a decentralized market might dominate a single market in terms of social efficiency. Our empirical findings on bid-ask spread heterogeneity across dealers, the relative price inefficiency of their quotes, and the process through which information gets revealed in the dealer network hopefully inspire future researchers to generate these patterns in equilibrium. This will enable us to judge market quality and identify appropriate regulation, if needed. We leave these questions for future research.
Editors: Stefan Nagel, Philip Bond, Amit Seru, and Wei Xiong
article.Appendix A: The EUR/CHF Crash:
In this appendix, we present descriptive statistics on quoting activity, prices, and bid-ask spreads, as well as information share estimates, for the EUR/CHF crash on January 15, 2015. For an in-depth analysis of human and algorithmic trading activity at EBS on the day of the crash, see Breedon et al. (2018).
Trade statistics: Before analyzing the generation and revelation of information, it is useful to present and discuss some standard trade statistics. This should be of interest in and of itself, because we are the first to have access to such comprehensive data on trading around the EUR/CHF crash. FigureA1depicts how quote activity, prices, EBS trade volumes, and the bid-ask spread developed over the trading day, including the precrash, crash, and postcrash periods. The figure yields several insights.
Most importantly, Panel A of FigureA1shows that price discovery seems to happen exclusively in the EBS market in the hour after the SNB announcement. Price quote activity freezes almost completely in the OTC market right after the SNB announcement. There is a discontinuous drop from slightly fewer than 100 quote updates per minute in the minutes before the announcement (across all eight OTC dealers) to virtually zero in the minutes right after it. EBS quote change intensity also shows a sharp drop, but not to zero: there are around 150 EBS quote changes per minute just before the announcement, dropping to around 50 in the minutes right after it. The rate appears to pick up steadily and reaches preannouncement levels one hour after the announcement. We interpret this postannouncement hour as an OTC market freeze and thus analyze information revelation only from 11:30 AM onward.23
Panel B of FigureA1reveals that it took about an hour for EBS to discover at what approximate level the free float exchange rate should be. TheFinancial Timescharacterized the situation as an “unexpected move [that] has left currency strategists struggling to work out where a freely floating franc should be trading.”24The EUR/CHF quote midpoint price drops from 1.20 to 0.85 in the minutes after the announcement but then recovers and stabilizes at around 1.05 in the first hour of trading. Price discovery continues afterwards and price changes remain highly volatile compared to later days, but extreme price swings no longer occur. The intense price discovery in the postannouncement hour is accompanied by heavy EBS trading. Both buyer- and seller-initiated volumes are more than 50 times higher in the postannouncement hour compared to the preannouncement hour.
Panel C of FigureA1reveals that the EBS bid-ask spread jumps up from a preannouncement level of 0.2 basis points to 2.68% in the minutes right after the announcement. It declines over the course of the day but remains elevated by a few basis points throughout. Once quote activity picks up again in the OTC market, roughly an hour after the announcement, the median spread across dealers is at approximately the same level as the EBS spread. This general spread pattern is consistent with elevated volatility levels and arguably reflects both the adverse selection risk and inventory risk that are both suddenly present when the exchange rate is allowed to float freely.
Information origination: TableAIpresents information shares for the same time intervals used in Figure6. The interdealer limit order market EBS is the only market that generates information in the hour immediately after the announcement, because the OTC dealer quote stream freezes. Once the OTC markets resume quote activity, EBS remains disproportionally important compared to its role in the baseline sample (see TableII). The EBSInfoShareranges between 44.93% and 88.53%, compared to a normal level of 28.51% (see TableII).
The table further shows that some OTC dealers' information shares vary wildly. For example, DLR1 contributes 7.74% from 12:10 to 12:30 PM, then 0.74% from 12:30 to 1:15 PM, and then 14.54% over the 2:15 to 3:15 PM period. Other dealers experience similar jumps. Private information seems to come in bursts for dealers (probably when a dealer actively interacts with his private clients).
One influential client is potentially the SNB itself. Given its central role in the event, it is likely that trading by SNB left a strong footprint in the data in the aftermath of the crash. However, investigation of the EUR/CHF crash by Breedon et al. (2018) shows that the empirical patterns are robust to removing (an estimate of) SNB trading that day. We therefore expect the effect of SNB trading to be small in our sample as well, but unfortunately we do not have proxies for SNB trading to verify our conjecture.
